title: HPEAK COMPREHENSIVE PROGRAM REVIEW FA18-SP22 (1) (Part 1)
text:
HPEAK (Health, Physical Education, Athletics, and Kinesiology)
Instructional Comprehensive Program Review
FALL 2018 – SPRING 2022
Principal Preparer: Charlie Roche
Contributors: Tim Frisbie, Ed Kephart, Tyler Knudsen, Noelle Collier, Kyle Heath, Lyndi Scholl, Jon Cox,
Julius Jones, Tevin Cadola, Todd Zeigler, Kevin Bradford, Zac Myers, and Kristy Johnson.
The Program Review Data Report:
1. DESCRIPTION OF PROGRAM:
Organization:
The organization of the HPEAK - Health, Physical Education, Athletics, and Kinesiology has changed
during the review period. We are currently comprised of six full-time faculty members. Five are head
coaches of intercollegiate athletic programs. Our program was fortunate enough to hire three new
faculty in the Fall of 2022. We have had as many as eight full-time faculty in our department at one time.
This is a welcome addition to our department. We also have four part-time faculty serving as head
coaches of six programs. In addition to teaching personnel, the HPEAK program is supported by one
administrative assistant, one equipment/athletic field specialist, and two athletic trainers. All program
members report to the new Dean position. The program has had several positions change since the Fall
of 2018 as noted by the *.
Full-Time Faculty: Tim Frisbie
Full-Time Faculty: Ed Kephart (Head Men’s Soccer Coach and Head Women’s Soccer Coach)
Full-Time Faculty: Tyler Knudsen (Head Football Coach) *Hired Fall 2020
Full-Time Faculty: Noelle Collier (Head Women’s Basketball Coach) *Hired fall 2022
Full-Time Faculty: Kyle Heath (Head Men’s Basketball Coach) *Hired Fall 2022
Full-Time Faculty: Lyndi Scholl (Head Women’s Volleyball Coach) *Hired Fall 2022
Part-Time Faculty: Jon Cox (Head Softball Coach)
Part-Time Faculty: Julius Jones (Head Men’s Track Coach and Head Women’s Track Coach)
Part-Time Faculty: Steve Pyles (Head Men’s & Women’s Cross-Country Coach) *Hired
Administrative Assistant III: Kevin Bradford *Hired August 2022
Equipment Specialist / Athletics Field Specialist: Todd Zeigler
Head Athletic Trainer: Zac Myers
Assistant Athletic Trainer: Kristy Johnson *Hired June 2022
Dean: Charlie Roche *Moved from Faculty July 2020 (changed from Director to Dean in July 2022)

-- 1 of 18 --

Primary Purpose:
Recruit and retain a sizable portion of the full-time students at COS (College of the Siskiyous)
Prepare students for transfer
Prepare students for degrees in Kinesiology and Athletic Training

-- 2 of 18 --

Whom you serve (including demographics)
The HPEAK program serves three groups of students, Kinesiology majors, Athletic Training majors, and
Intercollegiate Student Athletes. The HPEAK program serves a large cohort of underrepresented
students.
Enrollment Data

-- 3 of 18 --

Gender Data

-- 4 of 18 --

title: HPEAK COMPREHENSIVE PROGRAM REVIEW FA18-SP22 (1) (Part 2)
text:
Race Data
What kind of services you provide:
The HPEAK department offers courses in four different disciplines including health, physical education,
athletics, and kinesiology. We have a few general education courses that allow students to get to know
the discipline while completing a general education course. We have degree specific courses that support
the completion of the degrees and certificates offered in our area. We have activity lab courses that
support our intercollegiate athletics program.
How you provide them:
Non-credit courses
Degree Applicable courses
Transfer courses
Vocational courses
Describe how your curriculum is up-to-date and needs based.
Since our last program review, we have:
Submitted the Physical Education AA program sequence into eLuman
• Created a sequence for students
• Created PLO’s
• Created more degree options for students
Created new transfer level lecture courses for the program
• KINE 1003 – CPR, First Aid, and Emergencies

-- 5 of 18 --

• KINE 1004 – Psychology of Sport
• KINE 1005 – Sport and United State Society
• KINE 1006 – Sports Management
• HEA 1009 – Human Sexuality
Added DE addendum to all Off-Season PEMA courses (due to COVID)
Updated / Changed the prefix on a few courses
PEMA 1031 to KINE 1031 Sports Officiating
Archived courses that we are not using (we do not have the capacity/facilities)
• PEAC1004 - Beginning Basketball
• PEAC1007 - Beginning Volleyball
• PEAC1008 - Beginning Bowling
• PEAC1024 - Beginning Tennis
• PEAC1048 - Beginning Boxing
• PEAC1104 - Intermediate Basketball
• PEAC1107 - Intermediate Volleyball
• PEAC1108 - Intermediate Bowling
• PEAC1124 - Intermediate Tennis
• PEAC1148 - Intermediate Boxing
• PEAC1204 - Advanced Basketball

title: HPEAK COMPREHENSIVE PROGRAM REVIEW FA18-SP22 (1) (Part 4)
text:
2. EXTERNAL FACTORS WITH SIGNIFICANT IMPACT:
What external factors have a significant impact on your program?
Housing for students that live outside of Weed
• The city of Weed has limited off campus housing options for full-time students that are
out of the area.
• Our lodges are limited to 152 beds. 8 of the beds are reserved for RA jobs. This leaves
only 144 beds for full-time students.
• The limited availability of housing for our student populations has hindered our ability to
grow.
Food services
Lab space and Lab quality - Facilities
Financial Aid process from application to completion
Enrollment process from application to the first day of class / Orientation
Student Employment – Lack of student work student positions on campus
Competition from other community colleges that have more efficient processes, better housing
opportunities, and better facilities.
CCCAA (California Community College Athletic Association) Constitution and Bylaws for our
athletics courses limit the number of contact hours
3. PROGRESS ON OUTCOMES ASSESSMENT
Please summarize the progress your unit has made on program and/or course level SLO measures you
have applied since your last Program Review:
We have been tracking and analyzing student attainment of learning outcomes informally. It
informs large and minor changes we make to our courses regularly. However, we do not have
clarity on the current institutional process for tracking these assessments. We got our program
updated in eLuman during the fall of 2021. We are still waiting for a way to capture course
SLO’s and Program level assessment data within eLuman.
Please describe any program/course and/or instructional improvements made by your unit as a result of
the outcomes assessment process.
We have had some changes to our courses recently due to SLO assessment and the COVID-19
pandemic.
These changes include:
Clarifying assignment guidelines
Changing the amount of time devoted to certain subjects
Provide compliance with COVID testing requirements by external (CCCAA) and internal (COS)
bodies
Lab courses required modification due to testing and COVID tracking
Greatly modifying assignments to meet learning objectives in the online or remote environment
Please describe the program’s assessment plan for the next four years.
We need to engage in a meaningful and formalized process and will do so once that process has
been delineated. SLO and PLO Assessment direction and coordinator.

-- 8 of 18 --

b) A summary of the results of these measures.
Our area provides the college with one of the largest access points for underrepresented
students. Our area serves the largest cohort of underrepresented students on campus. We
operate in the oldest, most outdated laboratories on campus.
c) What did you learn from your evaluation of these measures, and what improvements have you
implemented, or do you plan to implement as a result of your analysis of these measures?
Are we as a college interested in supporting the sustainability of programs that serve
underrepresented students?
6. EVALUATION: Based upon and not repeating the descriptions you provided in Question 1 and the
responses provided in Questions 2 – 5, please provide an analysis of what is going well and why and
what is not going well and why, in any of the following areas that are relevant to your program:
a) Representativeness of population served
• See above
b) Alternative modes and schedules of delivery (e.g., online, hybrid, early morning, evening services)
• Block schedule has created some difficulty in scheduling courses. Our students are full-time and
need a full load. The early morning block could be utilized more by our faculty if we had outdoor
facilities that would allow for this. (Turf / Lights) This would allow us to schedule classes around
the other courses our students take.
c) Partnerships (internal and external)
• Working with our CTE programs in scheduling time in the Weight Room and Gym lab spaces for
use.
• We are working with our CTE programs to extend the contracts of our athletic training staff so
that CTE academy students can use the services provided by our athletics training staff.
d) Innovation and implementation of best practices
• Adding HUDL Focus to three lab spaces has made it better for our faculty to evaluate students in
labs
e) Efficiency in operations
• During Covid – moving weight room outside to allow lab classes to continue and reduce the risk
of Covid for lab classes.
• Evaluating lab class time slots and scheduling to work with other areas.
f) Efficiency in resource use
• We review every year for ways to become more efficient with our funding.
g) Staffing

-- 12 of 18 --

title: HPEAK COMPREHENSIVE PROGRAM REVIEW FA18-SP22 (1) (Part 8)
text:
• During the review period we have had several changes in our department. We have added three
new faculty, hiring a replacement athletic coordinator, opening an assistant field specialist, and
hired an assistant athletic trainer three times.
h) Participation in shared governance (e.g., do unit members feel they participate effectively in planning
and decision-making?)
• Our faculty and staff serve on several committees
• Our faculty often feel left out of meetings due to scheduling conflicts with teaching.
• We often schedule meetings in the afternoon when most of our faculty teach.
i) Professional development and training
• Our faculty are required to participate in flex activities.
• Our staff have the opportunity for professional development every year.
j) Compliance with applicable mandates
• All faculty and staff complete the required CCCAA compliance exam yearly
7. VISION. Tell us your unit’s vision: Where would you like your program to be four years from now?
Dream big while considering any upcoming changes (e.g., new buildings, labs, growth, changes in the
discipline, etc.)
We would like to be operating with 250 full-time students. We have created a new major sequence to
meet the needs of our students. We are extremely concerned about the ability to grow back to that level
with the current housing situation for students. We are hopeful that the new housing project will be
completed soon and allow our programs to grow back to 250+ students.
LAB SPACE
Artificial surface (LAB SPACE for PEMA / PEFI courses)
Football/Soccer stadium
Baseball Field
Softball Field
All three items would provide better success and retention for our students. All three items would
provide a safe space for students to conduct their labs in PEMA courses.
Poor weather conditions and limited resources for upkeep are a major downfall for our current lab
space.
Has been on the facilities master plan since 2000 (23 years!)
We can increase enrolment for our college with this facility.
More full-time students
More degree seeking students
More transfer students
Our programs are the largest cohort of underrepresented students on this campus
Indoor training facility LAB SPACE for PEMA / PEFI courses)
Currently the gym is used by 14 programs.
The gym is the most used classroom on our campus. The gym is also one of the oldest buildings on
campus. We have been operating above capacity for many years.
The indoor training facility has been on the facilities master plan since 2000 (23 years!) Poor weather
conditions and limited resources for upkeep are a major downfall for our current lab space.
We can increase enrolment for our college with this facility.
More full-time students
More degree seeking students

title: HPEAK COMPREHENSIVE PROGRAM REVIEW FA18-SP22 (1) (Part 9)
text:
More transfer students
Our programs are the largest cohort of underrepresented students on this campus
Dedicated building for classrooms, meeting rooms, offices, locker rooms, shower facilities, circuit
training weight room, Olympic lifting weight room, storage space, yoga, and dance space for HPEAK.
We are currently spread all over our campus.
We are operating above capacity.
We continue to add programs (Men’s Soccer, Women’s Soccer, Men’s Cross Country, Women’s Cross
Country) and have not had a plan for the infrastructure needed to support the sustainability of these
programs.
Adding additional programs has caused strain on all our infrastructure.
Other colleges that we visit have found a way to support the upgrade of these facilities.
Lack of updated facilities has impacted recruitment, retention, and student success.

program’s last Four-Year Action Plan.

title: HPEAK COMPREHENSIVE PROGRAM REVIEW FA18-SP22 (1) (Part 11)
text:
We have adjusted our curriculum to meet our student population's needs.
Kinesiology lecture courses created since last Program Review.
Submitted the Physical Education AA program sequence into eLuman
• Created a sequence for students
• Created PLO’s
• Created more options for students
• Created new transfer level lecture courses for the program. These courses have
provided more transferable lecture-based courses for our students.
• KINE1000 - Introduction to Kinesiology
• KINE1003 - CPR, First Aid, and Emergencies
• KINE1004 - Psychology of Sport
• KINE1005 - Sport and United States Society
• KINE1011 - Nutrition
• KINE1030 - Principles of Physical Fitness and Wellness
• HEA1009 - Human Sexuality
9. FOUR YEAR-ACTION PLAN AND CONTINUOUS QUALITY IMPROVEMENT PROPOSAL (GOALS,
OBJECTIVES, RESOURCES, AND ACTIONS):
Reflect on your responses to all the previous questions. Write a Four-Year Action Plan, entering the
specific program goals you have formulated to maintain or enhance your strengths, or to address
identified challenges. In writing your objectives and developing your resource requests, consider student
learning and program assessment results. Assign an overall priority to each goal and each objective. In
addition, enter any actions and/or resources required to achieve each objective. Then complete a CQIP
(Continuous Quality Improvement Proposal) for each new budget allocation request.
Goal 1: Ensure we are meeting the needs of our students in our area and within the college
• Work together as a department in scheduling courses, travel, lab space, marketing and
promotion of our program.
• Obtain funding to update lab space.
• Complete the hiring process of an athletic advisor.
• Track transfer students and goal completion in our area better.
• Use data to help guide our area to better serve our students
• Ensure that the curriculum is up to date.
Goal 2: Provide an access point for underrepresented students to college.
• Obtain funding for updating lab spaces in our area to help with recruiting underrepresented
students to our college at a higher rate.
• Use data to help guide the results of this student population.
Goal 3: Increase access to our lab spaces for members of our community and beyond
• Create a physical fitness lab that has the flexibility to meet the needs of everyone on campus
and our community.

-- 16 of 18 --

• Be diligent in recording student hours.
• Ensure all faculty have the resources needed to effectively teach these lab courses.
Goal 4: Evaluate the efficiency of our programs and explore program closure and program addition
• Programs we can add that help with retention and/or new students
